1: The command to eat the scroll is repeated — the double command (2:8 and Ezekiel 3:1) emphasizes the necessity of internalization before proclamation. melet bitnek (millēʾ biṭnĕkā): 'fill your stomach/belly with it.' The belly (beten) — the interior of the person — must receive the scroll completely before the mouth can speak. The bodily metaphor is deliberate: the word of God must become part of the prophet's physical existence, not merely an external message he relays. Cf. Jeremiah's word as 'fire shut up in my bones' (Jeremiah 20:9).
2: Ezekiel opens his mouth and the divine hand feeds him the scroll. The passive construction — 'he gave me the scroll to eat' — maintains the divine initiative: Ezekiel does not choose to eat, he is fed. The word of God is not self-generated by the prophet but received and then re-expressed. This is the model for all biblical inspiration: the human vessel receives what comes from outside.
3: In my mouth it was like honey (devash, dĕḇaš) for sweetness. The sweetness of the scroll is a genuine and full sweetness, not ironic — the word of God is intrinsically delightful even when its content is judgment (cf. Psalms 19:10: 'sweeter than honey and drippings of the honeycomb'; Psalms 119:103). The prophet who tastes the sweetness of the word can proclaim judgment without bitterness — the tragedy of the message is held within the sweetness of being entrusted with it.
4: 'Go to the house of Israel and speak my words to them' — the commission restated after the scroll-eating. The message that Ezekiel was caused to eat is the same message he must now deliver: there is no gap between the received word and the proclaimed word. The digested scroll becomes the prophetic speech. This is the ideal of prophetic integrity: the prophet speaks not his own thoughts about God but the very words he has internalized.
5: 'You are not being sent to a people of foreign speech (shefat am, śĕp̄at ʿam) and difficult language (lishan kaved, lāšôn kābēd).' The 'heavy tongue' (literally 'heavy language') is the same idiom Moses used for his own speech impediment (Exodus 4:10: kĕbad peh, heavy of mouth). Ezekiel is not sent to those whose language he cannot understand — his commission is specifically to his own people who share his language but refuse to understand.
6: 'If I had sent you to foreign peoples of unfamiliar speech and difficult language, they would have listened to you.' The shocking comparison: pagan foreigners would have been more receptive to the divine word than covenant Israel. This is the same pattern Jesus invokes (Matthew 11:21-24: Tyre and Sidon would have repented) and Paul develops (Romans 1:18-32; the Gentiles who have the law written on their hearts). The accessibility of the word to outsiders heightens the culpability of insiders who resist it.
7: 'But the house of Israel will not listen to you because they will not listen to me — for all the house of Israel is hazak-metsach (ḥĕzaq-mēṣaḥ): hard of forehead (impudent/brazen) and qesheh-lev (qĕšê-lēb): stubborn of heart.' Two anatomical metaphors: forehead (public face of stubborn refusal to bow) and heart (inner decision-making center). The double hardness means they are resistant both externally (will not appear to yield) and internally (do not want to yield). The compound picture is of complete spiritual calcification.
8: 'But I have made your face as hard as their faces, and your forehead as hard as their foreheads.' The divine response to Israel's brazen resistance: God hardens the prophet's face to match — not in stubbornness of sin but in unflinching proclamation. natatih paneika chazaq linegev penehem (nātattî p̄ānêkā ḥāzāq lĕneged p̄ĕnêhem): 'I have made your face strong against their face.' The prophet's prophetic courage is not natural human bravery but God-given resistance to human intimidation.
9: shamir (šāmîr): 'adamant/emery' — the hardest stone known in antiquity, used to cut other stones. Your forehead like adamant, harder than flint (challamish, ḥallāmîš). The escalation: adamant harder than flint. The prophet who must stand against the stony resistance of Israel is given a forehead of harder stone — divine hardness against human hardness. 'Do not be afraid of them and do not be dismayed at their looks, for they are a rebellious house.'
10: 'Son of man, take all my words into your heart (levaveka, lĕbābĕkā) and hear with your ears.' The double command — heart first, then ears — reverses the ordinary sequence (ears hear, then heart receives). The divine word requires not just auditory reception but internalization: the prophet who will proclaim must first have the word lodge in his heart-center. This is the basis for the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33 (law written on hearts) which Ezekiel parallels in Ezekiel 36:26-27.
11: 'Go to your own people, to the exiles, and speak to them. Say to them, “Thus says the Lord GOD” — whether they listen or they refuse (yishmehu veIM yichdalu).' The phrase 'whether they listen or not' recurs (Ezekiel 2:5, 7; Ezekiel 3:11) as the theological framework for prophetic ministry independent of results. Ezekiel is sent first to the exiles — those already in Babylon — before the fall of Jerusalem (in 586 BCE). His ministry is among the community that sees themselves as abandoned by God, teaching them to find the divine presence even in exile.
12: The Spirit lifted me up (vatisani ruach, wattissāʾēnî rûaḥ), and behind me I heard a great rumbling (qol raash gadol, qôl raʿaš gādôl): 'Blessed be the glory of YHWH from his place' (baruch kevod YHWH mimqomo). The doxology is the response of the chariot-beings to the divine departure — the chariot moves when the Spirit lifts the prophet. Baruch kevod YHWH is a formal liturgical doxology; mimqomo (from his place) — an unusual phrase that may mean 'wherever he is,' reflecting the new theology of the mobile divine presence that is not confined to Zion.
13: The sound of the living creatures' wings touching one another, and the sound of the wheels beside them — the full auditory impact of the departing chariot, described as a great roar. The prophet experiences the sound of the divine departure even as he is being sent on mission. The departure of the chariot from the Chebar to commission a prophetic mission is itself a theological statement: the divine glory moves to empower the prophet who moves toward the exiles.
14: vaelech mar uvachamat ruach (wāʾēlēk mār ûvaḥămat rûaḥ): 'I went in bitterness (mār) and in the heat/rage of the Spirit (ḥămat rûaḥ).' The prophet's emotional state: bitter, hot with prophetic compulsion. Mār (bitter) is not spiritual bitterness or resentment but the visceral pain of one tasked with proclaiming judgment to his own people. yad YHWH alai hazqa (yad Yhwh ʿālay ḥāzĕqâ): 'the hand of YHWH was strong upon me' — the divine compulsion that overcomes the prophet's personal grief and sets him in motion.
15: Arrived at Tel-abib (tel aviv, tel ʾābîb): 'mound of the spring flood' (probably from Akkadian tēl abubi, mound of the great flood — a tell raised by the primeval flood-disaster; later the name of the first Hebrew city in modern Israel). The prophet sits with the exiles and remains among them overwhelmed (mashim, maštîm or mĕšōmēm) for seven days. The seven-day stunned silence before speaking parallels the seven-day mourning/ordination period (Leviticus 8:33) — the prophet undergoes a kind of ordination through communal sitting before he speaks.
16: At the end of seven days, the word of YHWH came to Ezekiel — the prophetic reception formula that will introduce the new commission of watchman. The seven-day waiting period is ended not by the prophet's initiative but by the word's arrival: divine speech determines the timing of prophecy, not the prophet's readiness. This is consistent with Ezekiel's overall pattern of passive reception followed by active proclamation.
17: tsopheh (ṣōp̄eh): 'watchman.' The specific term for the sentinel stationed on the city wall to watch for approaching threats. Ezekiel is appointed as a watchman (nātattîkā ṣōp̄eh: I have appointed you a watchman) for the house of Israel. The watchman metaphor defines his role: not priest (though he is one), not king, not intercessor, but a sentinel responsible for warning. The watchman analogy establishes the life-or-death stakes of prophetic proclamation: failure to warn means complicity in the death of the warned.
18: 'If I say to the wicked, “You will certainly die,” and you give no warning — that wicked person will die for his iniquity, but I will require his blood from your hand.' damim (dāmîm): 'blood (guilt)' — the plural of dām used for bloodguilt, legal responsibility for death (Numbers 35:27; 2 Samuel 16:8). The prophet who fails to warn is held legally responsible for the spiritual blood of the unwarned. Paul uses this exact framework in Acts 20:26: 'I am innocent of the blood of all of you, for I did not shrink from declaring the whole counsel of God' — Paul fulfills the watchman obligation of Ezekiel 3:18.
19: 'But if you warn the wicked and he does not turn from his wickedness — he will die in his iniquity, but you will have saved your life (nafshecha hitsalta, nap̄šĕkā hiṣṣaltā).' The watchman's faithfulness determines his own fate, not the wicked's choice. The double outcome: the unrepentant wicked person dies, but the faithful warner lives. This is the covenantal principle of individual moral responsibility — each person bears their own iniquity, but the prophet who fails to warn bears theirs alongside his own.
20: 'And when a righteous person turns from their righteousness and commits iniquity, and I place a stumbling block before them, they will die.' mikhshol (mikšôl): 'stumbling block' — YHWH placing a stumbling block before the would-be sinner who turns from righteousness is a concept that has generated theological debate (divine permission vs. divine causation of sin). The context in Ezekiel emphasizes individual responsibility: the turning from righteousness is the sinner's own act; the stumbling block is the consequence, not the cause. Paul uses proskomma (stumbling block) in Romans 14:13 and 1 Corinthians 8:9 in the related context of causing others to fall.
21: 'If you warn the righteous not to sin, and they take warning and do not sin, they will surely live because they took warning — and you will have saved your life.' The fourfold case structure (wicked/unwarned, wicked/warned, righteous-who-falls/unwarned, righteous/warned) establishes the comprehensive scope of the watchman's responsibility. The final case — the righteous who is warned and remains righteous — is the positive conclusion: the ministry of warning can preserve righteousness as well as call back the wicked.
22: yad YHWH (yad Yhwh) again — the third occurrence in the book (Ezekiel 1:3; Ezekiel 3:14; Ezekiel 3:22), marking the beginning of the next visionary episode. The divine hand comes upon Ezekiel in the valley (biq'ah, biqʿâ) — the same Hebrew word used for the Valley of Dry Bones in Ezekiel 37. The valley is the place of open divine encounter outside the city, outside the temple precincts — reinforcing the theme of mobile divine presence.
23: The glory of YHWH was there (kevod YHWH) — just like the vision at the Chebar (Ezekiel 1). The second appearance of the divine chariot-glory confirms the identity of the Chebar vision and establishes continuity: Ezekiel has not been abandoned between the initial call-vision and this commission to silence and confinement. He falls on his face as before (Ezekiel 1:28) — the prostration of the finite creature before infinite holiness.
24: The Spirit entered into him (vatavoh bi ruach) and set him on his feet — as in Ezekiel 2:2, the Spirit must enable standing before the word can be received. 'Go, shut yourself inside your house.' The divine command to confinement: after seven days of communal sitting among the exiles, now a period of isolation. The stages of Ezekiel's ministry: vision → commission → communal sitting → isolation → sign-acts. Each stage is divinely ordered.
25: 'Cords will be put on you and you will be tied with them so you cannot go out among the people.' The physical confinement of the prophet — he will be bound by the community he was meant to serve. Whether literal or figurative (the social pressure of a hostile community keeping him confined), the bound prophet is the sign of a bound nation: Israel is in exile (the bonds of galut), and Ezekiel's immobility enacts the nation's captivity.
26: 'I will make your tongue stick to the roof of your mouth — you will be silent (ne'elem, neʾĕlam) and unable to rebuke them.' The divine-imposed dumbness (illem) will be Ezekiel's condition until Jerusalem falls (Ezekiel 33:22: 'my mouth was opened and I was no longer silent'). The dumbness is not total — Ezekiel speaks throughout (his oracles continue) — but he cannot speak freely for himself or as an intercessor (tokiah, rebuke/argue their case). His dumbness is the sign that the divine case against Israel is closed to human appeal.
27: 'But whenever I speak to you, I will open your mouth, and you will say to them, “Thus says the Lord GOD.” Whoever listens, let him listen; and whoever refuses, let him refuse — for they are a rebellious house.' The final restatement: the prophetic word depends entirely on divine initiative ('when I speak to you, I will open your mouth') and is entirely unconditioned by audience response ('whoever listens, let him; whoever refuses, let him'). The watchman has delivered the warning; the outcome belongs to those who hear.
